today I'm going to share a story with you about a solution to climate change I helped create that integrates three topics you've likely never heard in the same sentence real estate development school children and worms I promise you they're all deeply interconnected I'd like to begin today by introducing you to a problem that existed in my community and from a small town in Connecticut and near my former high school is this old manufacturing facility called the Gilbert embedded wire mill site the wire mill was an operation from 1848 to 1998 manufacturing various wire products what's really interesting about this company was how innovative they were they actually invented wire mesh there so many people say that they invented summer because their invention allowed people to open up their windows and with their brand new window screens enjoy the warm summer air without letting mosquitoes and other bugs into their homes unfortunately today the mill lies derelict in the center of town it's uh nopper a tional and it's contaminated from the 150 years of metal manufacturing that happened there classifying the site as what's called a brownfield we're not creating jobs there we're not generating tax revenue and we've really lost our heritage of the industrious working spirit that once existed in georgetown so five years ago recognizing this problem a local artist for my community Jayne Philbrick decided to work with this problem in a really unconventional way she decided to engage the town's most unlikely and most overlooked constituents the local schoolchildren so I arrived at my tenth grade math class a few months later to find an invitation on my desk inviting me to apply for an internship opportunity in art architecture and future planning the sheet read that my ideas would be examined developed reworked and maybe even realized curious it asked and I was especially when I met the two really cute Swedish architecture students that Jane had brought along to work with us so I applied fast forward a few months later and here is a photo of a group of rewire interns from the local middle and high schools I'm in the back row on the left each high schooler was assigned to be principal of a different research vertical related to what we envisioned for the site this included food and farming architecture green energies transportation and fashion and we were encouraged to share knowledge with each other and cross-pollinate ideas as much as possible over the next couple of years we've sketched and we built models and we built even bigger models and to bring it full circle the studio that we performed all of this working is actually the same building that the owners of the wire mill had constructed a year earlier as a schoolhouse for the children of the mill workers building on our research with the younger children we the high schoolers created a master plan of what we envision for the site the most novel part of this whole experience is that we were asked for the first time in our lives what we wanted to see built in our community it would seem that this is a sensible question to ask our young people but we rarely ever ask in a meaningful way from the two years of work with the rewire school children and many other experts that we engaged along the way rewire led to the creation of a real estate development company called till of which I am co-founder I encourage you to think about who is meaningfully engaged in the real estate development process do you have a meaningful say and what's built around you our process works to engage all stakeholders in the real estate development process including architects yes finance years yes politicians of course but we also engage the future of our societies the extensible beneficiaries of what we're going to build our young people but from our four years of pre development work one theme emerged as particularly relevant to all of us there was one aspect of the building process that we all felt uncomfortable about and that we needed to find a solution for before we moved forward with our vision for the site and that one fact is that currently 40 percent of the world's energy is used in buildings this makes buildings the largest contributor to global greenhouse gas emissions contributing to this problem we're repeating conventional methods of construction was not part of our vision for the world we wanted to grow up in you might be thinking Olivia can't you just solve that with some solar panels in a wind farm in part yes and that's certainly part of our development model but astoundingly what that 40% figure does not include are what we call embodied emissions and embodied emissions are those created and the manufacturing of construction materials for example the steel and concrete that we manufacture to pave over surfaces and to build buildings on top of them actually contribute many tons of co2 into our atmosphere every year and beyond that when we use things like concrete to pave over our surfaces we actually cause biological death of the soil under meaning that we're destroying the soils capacity to capture excess carbon in our atmosphere and store it deep underground and really stable carbon stores called carbon sinks and it's actually our earthworms that are most crucial and bringing that carbon from the atmosphere into the soil deep into the ground when we pave over our soil we kill those worms and all related microbial life thereby destroying soils capacity to act as these incredibly important carbon sinks so if we're to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and mitigate climate change we need a two-part solution we need to reduce energy consumption yes especially in the built environment with things like solar panels and wind farms but we also need to find ways to create additional carbon dioxide storage so the central question for our team then became wouldn't it be great if we could find a way to turn large-scale real estate development into sites for carbon dioxide storage as opposed to the carbon factories that they currently are how would one do that we'll use a method called phytoremediation which uses plants to not only take out the toxins in the soil but phytoremediation also allows the soil to continue sequestering carbon from the atmosphere and storing it in the soil in addition to practicing phytoremediation we won't be relying on steel or concrete to build in their place we will use a material called cross laminated timber or CLT and CLT is just a fancy name for a type of engineered wood that's actually a strong as steel but what's unique about CLT is that it's 50% sequestered carbon to put that into perspective some estimates calculate that a single five-story CLT building is able to cut emissions by levels equivalent to removing 600 cars from the road for a year so by combining phytoremediation and building raised above the ground out of cross laminated timber our team has created a model of real estate development that isn't contributing to climate change at all in fact our model is carbon negative meaning that it buys us time in climate change now you might be wondering Olivia is it really safe to live above these remediating landscapes and what I would say in response is that this is about shifting our focus from removing any exposure to any hazard in our environment to thinking about the pathways by which were exposed to contaminants a quick analogy is what if I told you that you're going to be walking down the street and I was going to send a two-ton hurtling object past you at 40 miles per hour Olivia you would say please don't do that put up a barrier and protect me right and if you haven't guessed yet what I'd be describing is a sidewalk just as sidewalks protect us from cars living separate and above these landscapes protects us from the contaminants below and actually our research demonstrates that living and working in these fight over mediating brownfields is not only safe and possible but actually creates a healthier atmosphere than is the norm in urban conditions too close I'd like to go back that original line that encouraged me to apply to rewire in the first place it said your ideas will be examined developed reworked and maybe even realized what began as a problem specific to my community has transformed into a solution that can be applied globally to help slow down climate change there are two to three million brownfields in the United States alone it's likely that there's a brownfield not too far from where you live what if we turned every one of those two to three million brownfields and to economically productive carbon storage sites that's a world I want to live in thank you

And now, with some thoughts about how climate change is affecting the real estate industry. Mike Toffel, a professor of environmental management at Harvard Business School. MIKE TOFFEL: Climate change is affecting real estate development in a variety of ways. One thing that's far from settled is the role of flood insurance. In the US, about five million properties buy subsidized flood insurance from the federal government's National Flood Insurance Program. But the program is in trouble. Recent hurricanes, including Katrina in 2005, Sandy in 2012, and Harvey in 2017 have led to huge claims, and the program is increasingly in debt. And climate change is only increasing the risk and magnitude of flooding, placing more property at risk, which further burdens the program. Because government-provided flood insurance is subsidized, this means its premiums aren't high enough to accurately price risks.

That leaves the taxpayer on the hook, while property developers and property owners get the gains from engaging in risky behavior, like developing property in flood prone areas to enjoy seaside views, but don't pay the full cost of protecting themselves against flood risks. Like all subsidies, this leads to overconsumption of the subsidized behavior, including rebuilding in the same place after a flood occurs despite an increased probability that it'll get hit with another flood. Climate change is increasing the risks and costs of flood damage, which means the cost of the government subsidizing flood insurance is likely to rise. Arthur Segel argued that the government should step back from providing subsidized flood insurance. What would happen if this were to occur? First, the private sector might step in, though because it wouldn't be subsidized, insurance prices would be higher in these high risk areas.

That might encourage the development of more resilient infrastructure, and buildings might be required to meet more resilient building standards that private insurers might require as a condition for ensuring their property. Second, if the government stopped providing subsidized flood insurance, we might see the private sector developing a more sophisticated way of pricing risk. In a recent Harvard Business Review article entitled, How the Insurance Industry Can Push Us to Prepare for Climate Change, Matthew Kahn, Brian Casey, and Nolan Jones noted that private insurance companies could take advantage of big data and advances in geospatial sciences to better calculate risks and set prices that more accurately reflect those risks on a property by property basis, rather than setting the same price for all properties in a flood zone.

This form of price differentiation, common in other areas of insurance, could encourage a more targeted approach from the private sector regarding where to build and how much resilience to incorporate in a building's design. This price differentiation could also make it too expensive to build in the highest risk areas, which would steer development toward lower risk areas. Finally, for residents and owners of current properties already located in high risk areas, more expensive private insurance would likely lead some to abandon insurance, believing it to be too expensive. But if that happened, once a flood hit, the big question is whether the government would still bail them out. It could be that flood victims might be viewed as too many to fail, which could create political pressure to reimburse them anyway, which could unravel the private insurance market. To prevent this, the government might require them to purchase flood insurance, though again, that's a political fight.

And it's important to keep in mind that if the government threatened to leave this market, millions of property owners would be hurt, and the rest of the citizens would only gain a little. A classic collective action problem that makes it politically difficult to convince governments to stop providing flood insurance. And so I think it's hard to imagine that the US government will actually abandon the National Flood Insurance Program anytime soon. Meanwhile, the federal government is trying to improve the program in ways that can try to incorporate some of the sophistication that the HBR article discussed. A program called Risk Rating 2.0 is underway that seeks to have the government program more precisely assess and price risk using new maps and modeling techniques. This will result in less risky properties paying lower premiums and higher risk properties paying higher premiums. Opposition has already delayed its implementation for at least a year, to October 2021.

The flood insurance market in the US could also shift to approach as seen in other countries. First, the government could require insurers to offer policyholders the option to purchase supplemental coverage for flood damage, akin to what happens in Germany and Italy. Or insurers could offer bundled coverage, which combines flood insurance with protection against other risks, such as fire and windstorm. This would distribute risk across larger geographic areas and reduce the correlation between catastrophes. This type of bundling occurs in the UK, Spain, and Japan. A third approach is to have the government be a secondary insurer, offering compensation only after private insurance policies pay out. What are the implications of all this? Real estate investors need to keep an eye on how innovations in the flood insurance market play out as changes can have a big impact on real estate valuation.

At the same time, citizens should keep an eye on this too because the taxpayer cost of government subsidized flood insurance will rise as climate change brings flooding more often and more intensively. At some point, the citizenry might get tired of subsidizing the development of flood zones. When thinking about how climate change is affecting real estate, the role of insurance is one of the most interesting topics that hasn't yet made it into everyday conversation, but I think it's an area we should all be watching. You can subscribe on Apple Podcasts or wherever you listen. And please, leave us a review. We appreciate the feedback.

Joining us to discuss the climate challenges confronting the real estate industry are Arthur Segel, a real estate magnate, professor of management at Harvard Business School, and Bryan Koop, an executive vice president of Boston Properties, one of the largest commercial real estate development companies in the country, with a portfolio of nearly 50 million square feet of property.

MIKE TOFFEL: Climate change is affecting real estate development in a variety of ways. One thing that's far from settled is the role of flood insurance.
In the US, about five million properties buy subsidized flood insurance from the federal government's National Flood Insurance Program. But the program is in trouble.

Recent hurricanes, including Katrina in 2005, Sandy in 2012, and Harvey in 2017 have led to huge claims, and the program is increasingly in debt. And climate change is only increasing the risk and magnitude of flooding, placing more property at risk, which further burdens the program.

Because government-provided flood insurance is subsidized, this means its premiums aren't high enough to accurately price risks. That leaves the taxpayer on the hook, while property developers and property owners get the gains from engaging in risky behavior, like developing property in flood prone areas to enjoy seaside views, but don't pay the full cost of protecting themselves against flood risks.

Like all subsidies, this leads to overconsumption of the subsidized behavior, including rebuilding in the same place after a flood occurs despite an increased probability that it'll get hit with another flood.
Climate change is increasing the risks and costs of flood damage, which means the cost of the government subsidizing flood insurance is likely to rise.

Arthur Segel argued that the government should step back from providing subsidized flood insurance. What would happen if this were to occur? First, the private sector might step in, though because it wouldn't be subsidized, insurance prices would be higher in these high risk areas.

That might encourage the development of more resilient infrastructure, and buildings might be required to meet more resilient building standards that private insurers might require as a condition for ensuring their property.
Second, if the government stopped providing subsidized flood insurance, we might see the private sector developing a more sophisticated way of pricing risk.

In a recent Harvard Business Review article entitled, How the Insurance Industry Can Push Us to Prepare for Climate Change, Matthew Kahn, Brian Casey, and Nolan Jones noted that private insurance companies could take advantage of big data and advances in geospatial sciences to better calculate risks and set prices that more accurately reflect those risks on a property by property basis, rather than setting the same price for all properties in a flood zone.

This form of price differentiation, common in other areas of insurance, could encourage a more targeted approach from the private sector regarding where to build and how much resilience to incorporate in a building's design.
This price differentiation could also make it too expensive to build in the highest risk areas, which would steer development toward lower risk areas.

Finally, for residents and owners of current properties already located in high risk areas, more expensive private insurance would likely lead some to abandon insurance, believing it to be too expensive.

But if that happened, once a flood hit, the big question is whether the government would still bail them out. It could be that flood victims might be viewed as too many to fail, which could create political pressure to reimburse them anyway, which could unravel the private insurance market.

To prevent this, the government might require them to purchase flood insurance, though again, that's a political fight. And it's important to keep in mind that if the government threatened to leave this market, millions of property owners would be hurt, and the rest of the citizens would only gain a little.

A classic collective action problem that makes it politically difficult to convince governments to stop providing flood insurance. And so I think it's hard to imagine that the US government will actually abandon the National Flood Insurance Program anytime soon.
Meanwhile, the federal government is trying to improve the program in ways that can try to incorporate some of the sophistication that the HBR article discussed.
A program called Risk Rating 2.0 is underway that seeks to have the government program more precisely assess and price risk using new maps and modeling techniques.

This will result in less risky properties paying lower premiums and higher risk properties paying higher premiums. Opposition has already delayed its implementation for at least a year, to October 2021.

The flood insurance market in the US could also shift to approach as seen in other countries. First, the government could require insurers to offer policyholders the option to purchase supplemental coverage for flood damage, akin to what happens in Germany and Italy.

Or insurers could offer bundled coverage, which combines flood insurance with protection against other risks, such as fire and windstorm. This would distribute risk across larger geographic areas and reduce the correlation between catastrophes.
This type of bundling occurs in the UK, Spain, and Japan. A third approach is to have the government be a secondary insurer, offering compensation only after private insurance policies pay out.
What are the implications of all this? Real estate investors need to keep an eye on how innovations in the flood insurance market play out as changes can have a big impact on real estate valuation.

At the same time, citizens should keep an eye on this too because the taxpayer cost of government subsidized flood insurance will rise as climate change brings flooding more often and more intensively.

At some point, the citizenry might get tired of subsidizing the development of flood zones. When thinking about how climate change is affecting real estate, the role of insurance is one of the most interesting topics that hasn't yet made it into everyday conversation, but I think it's an area we should all be watching.
